name: Shinto Creation Myths
shortDescription: The Shinto creation myths detail the genesis of the world, the emergence of the first deities, and the formation of the Japanese archipelago.
description: The Shinto creation myths, found primarily in the Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters) and the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), narrate the formation of the cosmos and the birth of the kami (gods or spirits). The initial state is often described as a chaotic, formless void. From this void, the first kami spontaneously emerged in Takamagahara (the High Plain of Heaven). These primordial deities were generally sexless and solitary. Eventually, pairs of male and female kami appeared, marking a transition towards creation. The most significant of these pairs are Izanagi and Izanami, who are tasked with solidifying the floating land. They use the Amenonuhoko (heavenly jeweled spear) to stir the primordial ocean, and when they lift the spear, drops fall and form the island of Onogoro. Descending to this island, they build a palace and a heavenly pillar, and through a ritualistic dance, they procreate and give birth to the Japanese islands and many other kami. However, Izanami dies giving birth to the fire kami Kagutsuchi, and descends to Yomi (the underworld). Izanagi follows her but is horrified by her decaying form and flees, sealing the entrance to Yomi and performing purification rituals that give rise to further deities. The creation myths establish the divine origins of the Japanese imperial family and the close connection between the Japanese people and the land. They also explore themes of creation, death, purification, and the relationship between the human and divine realms. The myths emphasize the importance of ritual and proper order in maintaining harmony between these realms.
